recommendations.rationale: First-line and mandatory investigation for all presumptive TB cases. Identifies cavities, infiltrates, consolidation, and pleural effusion consistent with TB.
recommendations.india_context: Required under NTEP/NIKSHAY protocol. Available at all levels of healthcare including PHCs. Low cost and widely accessible.
recommendations.special_notes: Bilateral upper lobe involvement, cavitation, or miliary pattern are characteristic. Normal CXR does not exclude TB.

recommendations.rationale: Indicated when CXR is normal but clinical suspicion remains high (sputum-negative cases), for extent of disease, or to differentiate from other pathology.
recommendations.india_context: Reserve for complicated or diagnostically unclear cases. Consider radiation burden, especially in young patients.
recommendations.special_notes: Tree-in-bud pattern, centrilobular nodules, and cavities are highly suggestive of active TB.

recommendations.rationale: For evaluation and guided aspiration of pleural effusion. Useful in pregnancy as no radiation exposure.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred in pregnant patients or for guiding diagnostic/therapeutic pleural tap.

recommendations.rationale: May be needed to characterise mediastinal lymphadenopathy or rule out complications like empyema necessitans.
recommendations.india_context: Use HRCT first; add contrast only if specific mediastinal or vascular involvement is suspected.
recommendations.special_notes: Check renal function before contrast. Avoid in contrast allergy.

recommendations.rationale: Not routinely indicated for pulmonary TB. Limited by respiratory motion artefacts.
recommendations.india_context: Costly and not widely available. Reserve for spinal/pericardial TB evaluation.

recommendations.rationale: Not indicated for routine TB diagnosis. TB lesions are FDG-avid and can mimic malignancy.
recommendations.india_context: Not recommended for TB workup. Only consider if malignancy co-exists and cannot be excluded.

recommendations.rationale: Essential for diagnosis, extent, severity assessment (CURB-65), and to identify complications (effusion, abscess).
recommendations.india_context: Mandatory first investigation. Guides antibiotic choice and decision to admit. Follow-up CXR at 6 weeks to exclude underlying malignancy.
recommendations.special_notes: AP view acceptable if PA not feasible. Repeat CXR at 6 weeks if patient >40 years or smoker to rule out malignancy.

recommendations.rationale: For detecting and quantifying parapneumonic effusion, guiding diagnostic/therapeutic aspiration.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred over CT for effusion assessment. No radiation, bedside feasible.

recommendations.rationale: Indicated if CXR is non-diagnostic, clinical deterioration despite treatment, or suspicion of complication (abscess, empyema, malignancy).
recommendations.india_context: Do not use routinely. Reserve for complicated or non-resolving pneumonia.
recommendations.special_notes: CT is more sensitive than CXR for early infiltrates and complications.

recommendations.rationale: Not indicated for routine CAP evaluation.
recommendations.india_context: Not recommended. Use CXR or CT.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for characterising pulmonary mass — size, margins, density, enhancement, lymphadenopathy, and staging.
recommendations.india_context: Essential before any further workup or biopsy planning. Should include chest + upper abdomen.
recommendations.special_notes: Extend scan to include adrenals and liver for initial staging. Use thin slices (1–1.5 mm).

recommendations.rationale: Initial investigation and for surveillance. May miss lesions <1 cm or in hidden areas.
recommendations.india_context: Always start with CXR. Low cost, widely available.
recommendations.special_notes: If CXR abnormal, proceed to CT. Normal CXR does not exclude small or central lesions.

recommendations.rationale: Staging — detects mediastinal nodal involvement, distant metastases. Guides resectability assessment.
recommendations.india_context: Use for staging after tissue diagnosis, not for initial diagnosis. Available in tier-1 cities.
recommendations.special_notes: False positives in TB/granulomatous disease (common in India). Interpret with caution.

recommendations.rationale: For chest wall, mediastinal, or superior sulcus (Pancoast) tumour assessment. Better soft tissue delineation.
recommendations.india_context: Use selectively for specific indications.

recommendations.rationale: For peripheral masses abutting pleura — can guide biopsy.
recommendations.india_context: Useful for USG-guided biopsy of peripheral lesions.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis. Directly visualises thrombus in pulmonary arteries. Rapid, widely available.
recommendations.india_context: First-line definitive investigation in haemodynamically stable patients with intermediate/high pre-test probability.
recommendations.special_notes: Use WELLS or Geneva score for pre-test probability. Avoid in contrast allergy or severe renal failure.

recommendations.rationale: Initial investigation to exclude other diagnoses (pneumothorax, pneumonia). Usually normal or non-specific in PE.
recommendations.india_context: Always obtain as first step. Westermark sign, Hampton's hump are classic but rare.
recommendations.special_notes: Normal CXR with hypoxia in a breathless patient should raise PE suspicion.

recommendations.rationale: Bedside assessment of right heart strain in haemodynamically unstable patients. May show thrombus in main PA.
recommendations.india_context: Use as first-line in ICU/emergency setting when patient is too unstable for CTPA.
recommendations.special_notes: Normal echo does not exclude PE. Use to risk stratify confirmed PE.

recommendations.rationale: Alternative when CTPA is contraindicated. Preferred in pregnancy (lower fetal radiation than CTPA).
recommendations.india_context: Use when contrast is contraindicated or in pregnancy. Requires normal CXR for interpretation.
recommendations.special_notes: Low specificity in patients with underlying lung disease.

recommendations.rationale: Alternative to CTPA when contrast is contraindicated and VQ scan not available. Technical challenges limit reliability.
recommendations.india_context: Not widely available. Use only in specialized centres.

recommendations.rationale: Most sensitive for rib fractures, pneumothorax, haemothorax, aortic injury, pulmonary contusion. Superior to CXR.
recommendations.india_context: Should be done in all significant chest trauma. Include CT angiography if aortic injury suspected.
recommendations.special_notes: Part of trauma CT whole body protocol (polytrauma). Use MDCT.

recommendations.rationale: First-line portable investigation in trauma bay. Identifies pneumothorax, haemothorax, mediastinal widening.
recommendations.india_context: Immediate portable AP CXR as part of primary survey in all trauma patients.
recommendations.special_notes: AP supine view may miss up to 50% of pneumothoraces. Follow with CT.

recommendations.rationale: Bedside — detects pneumothorax (eFAST), haemothorax, pericardial tamponade rapidly.
recommendations.india_context: Integral part of trauma assessment. Faster than CT for unstable patients.
recommendations.special_notes: eFAST: Extended FAST includes lung sliding assessment for pneumothorax.

recommendations.rationale: First-line. Detects effusion >200 mL. Identifies laterality, associated lung/mediastinal pathology.
recommendations.india_context: Start with CXR. Always include PA and lateral views.

recommendations.rationale: Best for quantification, characterisation (simple/complex/septated), and guiding aspiration/biopsy. More sensitive than CXR.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred guidance tool for all pleural procedures. No radiation.
recommendations.special_notes: Can detect effusions as small as 20 mL. Identifies pleural thickening and nodularity.

recommendations.rationale: For determining underlying cause — pleural malignancy, empyema, mediastinal pathology, underlying lung disease.
recommendations.india_context: Order after initial USG-guided tap and fluid analysis. Critical for excluding malignancy.
recommendations.special_notes: Best done after drainage for better lung visualisation.

recommendations.rationale: First-line and sufficient for diagnosis in most cases. PA erect shows visceral pleural line, lung edge, and contralateral tracheal deviation in tension pneumothorax. Quantifies size.
recommendations.india_context: Available everywhere. Tall, thin young males most commonly affected (primary). In India, secondary pneumothorax from TB cavitation is also common. Never delay treatment for imaging in tension pneumothorax.
recommendations.special_notes: BTS size: Small <2 cm rim, Large ≥2 cm rim. Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis — treat before imaging if haemodynamically compromised.

recommendations.rationale: Detects underlying blebs/bullae (secondary cause), occult pneumothorax not visible on CXR, and complications. Essential before surgical intervention (VATS pleurodesis).
recommendations.india_context: CT is recommended before VATS in India. Also identifies underlying TB or COPD as secondary causes.
recommendations.special_notes: Identify number, size, and location of blebs/bullae for surgical planning. Also detects contra-lateral lesions.

recommendations.rationale: Bedside point-of-care ultrasound is highly sensitive for pneumothorax. Absent lung sliding + absent B-lines + lung point sign is diagnostic. Faster than CXR in ICU/emergency.
recommendations.india_context: POCUS increasingly adopted in Indian emergency departments and ICUs. Ideal for ventilated ICU patients where CXR is suboptimal.
recommendations.special_notes: Sensitivity >90% with POCUS. Lung point sign is pathognomonic. M-mode: stratosphere sign (no lung sliding) vs seashore sign (normal).

recommendations.rationale: First-line for mediastinal mass characterisation. Identifies location (anterior/middle/posterior), density (solid, cystic, fat, calcification), vascularity, and relationship to adjacent structures.
recommendations.india_context: Anterior mediastinal masses: 4 Ts — Thymoma, Teratoma, Terrible lymphoma, Thyroid. Lymphoma is most common in young Indian adults. TB lymphadenopathy (middle mediastinum) also common.
recommendations.special_notes: 4Ts rule: Anterior — Thymoma, T-cell lymphoma, Teratoma/GCT, Thyroid. Middle — lymphoma, TB, bronchogenic cysts. Posterior — neurogenic tumours (Schwannoma, Neuroblastoma).

recommendations.rationale: Superior for posterior mediastinal neurogenic tumours (spinal canal extension), vascular masses, and in children to avoid radiation. Better soft tissue characterisation than CT.
recommendations.india_context: Use for neurogenic tumours (Schwannoma, ganglioneuroma) to assess spinal canal involvement. Preferred in children.
recommendations.special_notes: T1/T2 characterisation: Lipoma (T1 bright), Cyst (T2 bright), Fibrous tissue (T2 dark). DWI for lymphoma (restricted diffusion).

recommendations.rationale: Essential for lymphoma staging and post-treatment response assessment (Deauville criteria). Helps distinguish lymphoma from thymoma in anterior mediastinal masses.
recommendations.india_context: Available at major oncology centres. Lymphoma is very common in young Indian adults. FDG PET is standard for lymphoma staging.
recommendations.special_notes: Very high FDG uptake = lymphoma or aggressive tumour. Low/absent uptake = thymoma, teratoma, or benign lesion.

scenarios.title: Empyema / Complicated Parapneumonic Effusion
symptoms: failure to improve after pneumonia treatment

recommendations.rationale: First-line assessment. Identifies effusion, consolidation, and hydropneumothorax. Loculation may appear as D-shaped opacity not shifting on decubitus view.
recommendations.india_context: TB is the most common cause of pleural empyema in India. Post-pneumonic empyema also common. Prompt diagnosis and drainage are critical.
recommendations.special_notes: Lateral decubitus view helps assess free vs loculated effusion. Failure to layer out on decubitus = loculated.

recommendations.rationale: Real-time guidance for thoracocentesis and chest drain placement. Identifies septations, echogenic debris (exudate/pus), and guides needle trajectory. Superior to CT for procedural guidance.
recommendations.india_context: First-line for all pleural procedures in India. Reduces complications of blind thoracocentesis. Portable ultrasound available in most Indian hospitals.
recommendations.special_notes: Septated/echogenic fluid = exudate/empyema. Anechoic free-flowing = transudate. US-guided drain placed in most echogenic, most accessible location.

recommendations.rationale: For complex or multiloculated empyema, failed drainage, or when surgical decortication is planned. Shows split pleura sign, loculations, thickened enhancing pleura, and underlying lung.
recommendations.india_context: CT is essential before surgical decortication. Also helps identify fistula (bronchopleural fistula) and underlying TB or malignancy.
recommendations.special_notes: Split pleura sign (enhancing visceral and parietal pleura separated by fluid) is characteristic of empyema. Identifies loculations not amenable to simple drainage.

scenarios.title: Diffuse Parenchymal Lung Disease / Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD)

recommendations.rationale: Initial investigation. Identifies distribution (upper, lower, bilateral), honeycombing, reticular opacities, ground-glass, and pleural changes. Normal CXR does not exclude early ILD.
recommendations.india_context: Sarcoidosis presenting as bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and ILD is common in India. Silicosis from mining and stone-cutting is an occupational ILD prevalent in specific regions.
recommendations.special_notes: Bilateral lower lobe fibrosis = UIP (IPF). Upper lobe = sarcoidosis, HP, silicosis. Mid-zone = NSIP.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for ILD characterisation. Identifies pattern (UIP/NSIP/DIP/COP/HP/sarcoid), distribution, and activity. Can guide biopsy site selection. Essential for all suspected ILD.
recommendations.india_context: HRCT is mandatory for ILD workup in India. Low-dose HRCT protocol reduces dose significantly. TB must always be excluded (HRCT may show TB mimicking ILD).
recommendations.special_notes: UIP pattern (basal-predominant honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, subpleural) = probable IPF. Ground glass predominant = NSIP, HP. Upper lobe nodules + hilar lymph nodes = sarcoidosis.
contraindicated_if: pregnancy (use standard dose only if essential — low-dose HRCT preferred)

recommendations.modality: Chest CT with contrast (for sarcoidosis / lymphoma)
recommendations.rationale: When lymphadenopathy is prominent (sarcoidosis, lymphoma) or vascular involvement suspected. Not routinely needed for standard ILD workup.
recommendations.india_context: Use when bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy needs characterisation (TB vs sarcoidosis vs lymphoma).
